_note: Difficulty is calibrated for a FRESHMAN/SOPHOMORE audience (CS1/CS2, just starting data structures), NOT the junior/senior job-seeker baseline the source (Byte by Byte) assumes. Problems that need trees / linked lists / stack-design or a tricky no-extra-structure constraint were moved up to medium/hard. `requires` lists the background a problem assumes, shown in the UI so a student knows before opening it.

questions.title: Find Duplicates
questions.prompt: Given an array of integers where each value 1 <= x <= len(array), write a function that finds all the duplicates in the array.
hints: Scan the list from left to right and remember values you have already seen.
When a value appears again, it belongs in the duplicate result.
Use a second set for duplicates so the same duplicate is not added twice.
questions.starter_code:
def find_duplicates(nums):
    # Return a list of the values that appear more than once in nums.
    # (Order does not matter.)
    return []

questions.title: Anagrams
questions.prompt: Given two strings, write a function to determine whether they are anagrams.
hints: Two anagrams must have the same length and the same letters.
Count how many times each character appears in the first string.
Subtract counts using the second string; if any count is wrong, they are not anagrams.

questions.title: Swap Variables
questions.prompt: Given two integers, write a function that swaps them without using any temporary variables.
hints: This problem is about changing two values without storing a third value.
One option is arithmetic: add them, then subtract to recover each original value.
Another option is XOR, but arithmetic is easier to trace for a first pass.

questions.title: Gray Code
questions.prompt: Given two integers, write a function to determine whether or not their binary representations differ by a single bit.
hints: Use XOR to show which bit positions are different between the two numbers.
If exactly one bit is different, the XOR result has exactly one 1 bit.
A number with one 1 bit becomes 0 when you compute x & (x - 1).

questions.title: Number of Ones in a Binary Number
questions.prompt: Given an integer, write a function to compute the number of ones in the binary representation of the number.
hints: Look at one bit at a time, starting from the rightmost bit.
n & 1 tells you whether the rightmost bit is 1.
After checking a bit, shift the number right and keep counting until it becomes 0.

questions.title: String Compression
questions.prompt: Given a string, write a function to compress it by shortening every sequence of the same character to that character followed by the number of repetitions. If the compressed string is longer than the original, return the original string.
hints: A run is a group of the same character next to itself, like aaa.
Track the current character and how many times it has repeated.
When the character changes, add the completed character-count pair, then compare the final compressed string length with the original.
questions.starter_code:
def compress(s):
    # Compress runs of the same character to char+count. If the result is not
    # shorter than the original, return the original string.
    return s

questions.title: Fibonacci Number
questions.prompt: Given an integer n, write a function to compute the nth Fibonacci number (fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1).
hints: Start with the two base cases: fib(0) is 0 and fib(1) is 1.
Each later number is the sum of the two before it.
An iterative loop is safer than plain recursion because it avoids repeating the same work many times.
